metadata.title: Simulate Berkson's Paradox with Claude

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Berkson's paradox is the reason hospital studies keep finding that diseases are negatively correlated with each other — even when they are completely independent in the general population. The structure is always the same: two independent causes, one collider — the hospitalization decision — and a sample drawn only from hospitalized patients. The question this video answers: can you simulate that bias, quantify it, and explain the structure.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'two independent diseases look negatively correlated in hospital data'; Berkson's paradox; no terminal yet

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Berkson's paradox is the reason hospital studies keep finding that diseases are negatively correlated with each other — even when they are completely independen

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Build a Python simulation of Berkson's paradox: generate ten thousand individuals with independent binary Disease A at five percent and Disease B at five percent, apply collider selection — hospitalized if Disease A or Disease B — compute the correlation in the full population versus the hospital sample, call Claude for a structural explanation, and generate a Manim scene.

props.command: claude "Build a Python simulation of Berkson's paradox: generate N=10,000 individuals with independent binary Disease_A (5%) and Disease_B (5%); apply collider selection (hospitalized if Disease_A OR Disease_B); compute correlation in full population vs hospital sample; call Claude for structural explanation; generate Manim scene"

beats.narration_text: Read it. The selection rule is one line: hospitalized equals Disease A or Disease B. That OR gate is the collider. In the full population the two diseases are independent by construction — correlation near zero. In the hospital subset every non-diseased person has been removed, so having Disease A makes Disease B slightly less likely — if you got in without Disease B, you probably have Disease A. The negative correlation is pure selection.

props.code:
# berkson_sim.py — Claude Code output
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
N = 10_000
disease_a = np.random.binomial(1, 0.05, N).astype(bool)
disease_b = np.random.binomial(1, 0.05, N).astype(bool)

# collider selection rule
hospitalized = disease_a | disease_b

corr_full = np.corrcoef(disease_a, disease_b)[0,1]
corr_hosp = np.corrcoef(
    disease_a[hospitalized], disease_b[hospitalized]
)[0,1]
print(f'Full pop:  rho = {corr_full:.2f}')
print(f'Hospital:  rho = {corr_hosp:.2f}')

beats.narration_text: Run it. Left panel: the full population. Sixty dots scattered randomly — slate, gold, crimson for neither, Disease A, Disease B. Correlation near zero. Right panel: the hospital subset. Only the diseased dots remain. A trend line runs downward from left to right: correlation minus zero point four two. Same people. Same diseases. Different sample. The negative correlation is entirely an artifact of the selection collider.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: two-panel scatter — full population random (rho=0.02) vs hospital subset with downward trend (rho=-0.42)

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to extend the simulation: vary the hospitalization threshold from OR to AND and show how the bias changes. At the AND extreme — hospitalized only if both diseases — you get the opposite bias. The collider structure creates a spectrum of bias depending on how extreme the selection is.

props.command:
claude "update berkson_sim.py:
  -> sweep selection: OR, mixed, AND
  -> compute correlation at each threshold
  -> show bias direction flip"

beats.narration_text: The updated simulation sweeps the selection gate from OR through mixed thresholds to AND. At OR selection the correlation is minus zero point four. At AND selection the correlation flips to plus zero point six — opposite bias. The zero crossing is at roughly the average prevalence threshold. Any selection on the collider creates bias; the direction depends on how aggressive the selection is.
beats.visual_intent: slate: table showing selection gate, correlation value, and bias direction across three thresholds

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The updated simulation sweeps the selection gate from OR through mixed thresholds to AND. At OR selection the correlation is minus zero point four. At AND selec

beats.narration_text: So Berkson's paradox is collider bias in a specific setting — hospital selection. The lesson generalizes: any time your sample is drawn by a process that depends on both your exposure and your outcome, you have a collider at the selection stage and your within-sample correlations are biased. This is why study design precedes analysis.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'selection on collider = spurious correlation; OR gate = negative bias; AND gate = positive bias'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So Berkson's paradox is collider bias in a specific setting — hospital selection. The lesson generalizes: any time your sample is drawn by a process that depend

beats.narration_text: Your move: think about your own dataset. How were subjects recruited? Was there a selection criterion that depended on both the exposure and the outcome you are studying? If yes, you may have Berkson bias. The next reel covers sensitivity analysis — how much unmeasured confounding would it take to overturn your causal conclusion.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) examine your own selection mechanism, 2) test for collider bias, 3) proceed to sensitivity analysis reel
